title: Scripture & Divine Revelation
summary: God has spoken. The 66 books of the Old and New Testaments are the inspired, reliable, and sufficient Word of God — the only trustworthy guide for faith and life.
scripture_text: All Scripture is breathed out by God and profitable for teaching, for reproof, for correction, and for training in righteousness, that the man of God may be complete, equipped for every good work.
doctrine:
The Westminster Confession opens with Scripture because you need to establish how God speaks before you can say anything reliable about who he is. The doctrine of inspiration — theopneustos in Greek, meaning 'God-breathed' — means the human authors wrote under the guidance of the Holy Spirit, producing a text that is simultaneously fully human and fully trustworthy.

The Bible did not arrive in a single document dropped from the sky. It came through around 40 different authors across 1,500 years — and yet tells one coherent story from creation through fall through redemption to restoration. That kind of unity across that kind of diversity is itself evidence of a single intelligence behind it.

The canon — the list of books we accept as Scripture — is not a Catholic power play or a political decision. It is the church formally recognising what the Holy Spirit had already marked as authoritative. Scripture is self-authenticating: the Holy Spirit seals its authority in the heart of the person who reads it with an open mind.
islamic_contrast:
Islam's central charge against the Bible is tahrif — the claim that Christians and Jews corrupted and changed their scriptures over time, making a new revelation through Muhammad necessary.

This claim is historically indefensible. The manuscript record for the New Testament is the strongest of any ancient document — over 5,800 Greek manuscripts, with the earliest fragments dating within decades of the originals, five centuries before Islam arose. No serious textual scholar accepts the corruption thesis.

More damaging still: the Quran itself repeatedly affirms the Torah and Gospel as valid (Surah 5:47, 5:68, 10:94), and instructs Muhammad to consult the People of the Book if he has doubts. If those scriptures were already corrupted at the time of the Quran's composition, this instruction is completely incoherent.

Tahrif is not a historical finding. It is a theological necessity — invented to explain why the Quran contradicts the Bible.

title: The Nature of God
summary: God is the only true and living God — infinite, eternal, and unchanging in his being, wisdom, power, holiness, justice, goodness, and truth. He does not depend on anything outside himself to exist.
scripture_text: God said to Moses, 'I AM WHO I AM.' And he said, 'Say this to the people of Israel: I AM has sent me to you.'
doctrine:
The name God gives himself — I AM WHO I AM — is the most important thing he could have said about his own nature. He is not a God who came into existence, who depends on anything else, or who could cease to exist. He simply and absolutely is.

Theologians call this aseity — God's complete self-existence, his total independence from everything else. He is not the greatest being in the universe. He is the ground of existence itself — the reason there is anything at all rather than nothing.

This metaphysical uniqueness is what underlies all his other qualities: his necessity (he cannot not exist), his eternity (he is outside time), his omniscience (he knows all things), his omnipotence (he is unlimited in power), and his perfect goodness (he is the standard by which goodness is measured).

Critically, this God is also personal. He speaks, covenants, loves, acts, and reveals himself. The combination of infinite metaphysical power and genuine personal relationship is what makes Christianity unique among all the world's religions.
islamic_contrast:
Islam affirms God's transcendence — his complete otherness from creation — so radically that it effectively cuts God off from meaningful personal relationship with the world.

In Islamic theology, Allah's will is absolute and unconditioned. His love and justice are not expressions of a stable eternal nature — they are sovereign choices that could, in principle, be otherwise. This position is called divine voluntarism: God's will precedes and determines his nature, rather than the other way around.

The result is a God whose goodness is defined as whatever he wills — not a God whose will expresses what he eternally is.

Reformed theology insists on the opposite: God is not good because he chooses to be good. He is good because goodness flows necessarily from his eternal nature. He cannot lie, because he is truth. He cannot be cruel, because he is love. His commands are not arbitrary — they express who he is.

The God of the Westminster Confession and the God of Islamic theology are not different descriptions of the same being. The difference goes all the way down to their foundations.

title: The Holy Trinity
summary: There is one God who eternally exists as three distinct Persons — Father, Son, and Holy Spirit — equal in nature, power, and eternity. This is not three gods. It is the only account of God that can explain how God has always been love.
scripture_text: Go therefore and make disciples of all nations, baptizing them in the name of the Father and of the Son and of the Holy Spirit.
doctrine:
The Trinity did not emerge from Greek philosophical speculation — it emerged from the early church's unavoidable confrontation with the evidence. Jesus received worship. Jesus forgave sins. Jesus identified himself with the Father. The Spirit was also called God. The first Christians were Jewish monotheists who believed, based on what they had witnessed, that the one God of Israel exists in three Persons.

The Councils of Nicaea (325 AD) and Constantinople (381 AD) did not invent the Trinity. They gave precise language to what the apostolic church already believed and defended it against distortions.

The Trinity also resolves a philosophical problem that haunts every other monotheism: if God is love, and love requires an object, how can love be an eternal quality of God if there was no creation to love? The Trinity answers this — the Father has eternally loved the Son in the fellowship of the Spirit. Love is not something God acquired when he made the world. It is what he has always been.

1 John 4:8 does not say God loves. It says God IS love. That statement only makes sense if there is eternal relationship within the one God.
islamic_contrast:
The most common Islamic charge against Christianity is shirk — associating partners with God, which Islam considers the one unforgivable sin. The Trinity, in this view, turns Christianity into a form of polytheism.

But look carefully at what the Quran actually claims the Trinity is. Surah 5:116 presents the Trinity as Father, Jesus, and Mary. No Christian council, creed, confession, or catechism in the entire history of the church has ever taught this. Islam is arguing against a position no Christian has ever held.

The actual Christian doctrine — one God in three Persons — is a claim about the internal structure of the one divine being, not about three separate gods.

There is also a deeper problem Islam cannot avoid. Tawhid — the belief that God is absolutely one and undivided with no internal differentiation — means that before creation, Allah had no one to love. Love, on this view, is not an eternal quality of God but something God acquired when he created beings to love. A God who only learned to love when he made the world is not the eternal, unchanging God Islam claims him to be.

title: The Covenant of Grace
summary: After humanity fell into sin, God took the initiative to rescue us — not because we deserved it, but because of his grace. The entire Bible tells one story of this rescue, accomplished through Jesus.
scripture_text: I will put enmity between you and the woman, and between your offspring and her offspring; he shall bruise your head, and you shall bruise his heel.
doctrine:
The very first thing God does after the fall is make a promise — that one day a descendant of the woman will crush the serpent. This is the seed of the gospel, planted in the earliest pages of Scripture.

Everything that follows in the Bible — the covenant with Abraham, the law given to Moses, the kingdom of David, the words of the prophets — is the unfolding of this one promise. The Old Testament is not a failed religion replaced by a better one. It is a single story whose every chapter points forward to Jesus.

Salvation has always been by grace through faith. Abraham was counted righteous because he believed God's promise — centuries before the law was given. The law was never meant to save anyone; it was meant to show people their need for the one who would.

The covenant of grace means that God — not humanity — takes the first step toward rescue. We did not find God; God came looking for us. This is not how Islam describes God's relationship with humanity.
islamic_contrast:
Islam has no covenant theology and no doctrine of grace in the sense the Bible describes.

In Islamic theology, salvation is fundamentally about human effort — good deeds weighed against bad deeds on the Day of Judgment, with Allah's mercy as an additional and unpredictable factor. There is no concept of God taking the initiative to rescue humanity. There is no atoning substitute. There is no imputed righteousness — the idea that someone else's perfect record could be credited to your account.

The Islamic believer cannot know in this life whether they will be accepted. Even Muhammad said he could not guarantee his own place in Paradise.

Reformed theology locates assurance not in the believer's performance but in the covenant-keeping faithfulness of God and the finished work of Christ. The difference is not just a detail — it is the entire structure of how God relates to humanity.

title: The Incarnation of the Son
summary: The eternal Son of God became a real human being — born of the Virgin Mary, fully divine and fully human at the same time. Not God pretending to be human. Not a human elevated to divine status. Both, completely, in one Person.
scripture_text: And the Word became flesh and dwelt among us, and we have seen his glory, glory as of the only Son from the Father, full of grace and truth.
doctrine:
The Incarnation — God becoming human in Jesus — is the most remarkable claim in the history of religion. Not a divine being wearing a human disguise. Not a human being who achieved a kind of divine status. The eternal Son of God took on a complete and real human nature while remaining fully God.

The Council of Chalcedon (451 AD) gave this the most precise formulation ever achieved: one Person, two natures — divine and human — united without confusion, without change, without division, without separation. Each nature remains fully what it is.

This matters enormously for salvation. Only God can bear the infinite weight of sin's penalty. Only a human being can stand in humanity's place. A merely human Jesus saves no one. A God who never became human represents no one. The God-man — both fully — is the only one who can do what needed to be done.

This is not mythology. Paul's letter to the Philippians, written within 30 years of the crucifixion, describes the Son existing 'in the form of God' before taking on human flesh (Philippians 2:6-8). This is not a legend that developed over centuries — it is the earliest testimony.
islamic_contrast:
Islam categorically denies the Incarnation. Jesus (Isa) was a prophet — born of a virgin, performing miracles, highly honoured — but not divine. The idea of God taking on human flesh is, in Islamic theology, a category error bordering on blasphemy.

But consider what this denial costs. If Jesus was only a prophet who did not die for sin and did not rise from the dead, what exactly does his life accomplish? A prophet who cannot save is an inspiring historical figure at best.

The Christian response is that a God who is so sovereign and so free that he can enter his own creation without ceasing to be God is more transcendent, not less, than a God whose greatness depends on permanent distance.

There is also a historical problem. The Quran affirms that Jesus was born of a virgin and performed miracles including raising the dead. These are claims of a kind that demand explanation. A merely human prophet does not get a miraculous birth and miraculous powers — and then accomplish nothing salvific with them.

title: The Resurrection of Christ
summary: On the third day Jesus rose bodily from the dead — the same body that was crucified, now transformed and glorified. This is the central claim of the Christian faith. Everything depends on whether it actually happened.
scripture_text: And if Christ has not been raised, your faith is futile and you are still in your sins.
doctrine:
Paul's logic is stark and deliberate: if the Resurrection did not happen, Christianity is a lie and Christians are to be pitied above all people. He is not offering a spiritual metaphor. He is making a historical claim that can be investigated.

The minimal facts argument — built from data that most historians accept regardless of their personal beliefs — establishes five points: Jesus died by crucifixion; was buried in a known tomb; the tomb was found empty three days later; multiple individuals and groups reported seeing him alive afterwards; and the disciples were so transformed by this that they gave their lives for the claim.

The Resurrection uniquely explains all five facts simultaneously. No alternative theory — hallucination, theft, mistaken identity — accounts for all of them at once.

This is not blind faith. It is a historical conclusion from evidence. Paul's creed in 1 Corinthians 15:3-7, dated by scholars to within 2-5 years of the crucifixion, names eyewitnesses who were still alive when he wrote it. This is the kind of early, checkable testimony that historians rely on for any other ancient event.
islamic_contrast:
The Quran explicitly states that Jesus was not crucified and did not die — 'it was made to appear so' (Surah 4:157). This places Islam in direct conflict with the most well-documented event of ancient history.

The crucifixion is confirmed by Tacitus (a Roman hostile to Christianity), Josephus (a Jewish historian), the Babylonian Talmud (a Jewish text), and every strand of the New Testament tradition. The sources are diverse, independent, and many of them have no interest in supporting Christian claims.

The Quran's denial, written 600 years after the event, cites no historical sources. It is a theological assertion imposed onto history.

There is also a serious internal problem for Islam. If God made it appear that Jesus was crucified when he was not — if the entire early church was founded on what was effectively a divinely engineered illusion — then the God who did this is a deceiver. Islam claims its God is perfectly truthful. These two things cannot both be true.

title: The Problem of Sin and Why It Needed a Solution
summary: Sin is not just bad behaviour that needs correcting — it is a broken relationship with a holy God that carries a real penalty. Understanding this is what makes the cross make sense.
scripture_text: For the wages of sin is death, but the free gift of God is eternal life in Christ Jesus our Lord.
doctrine:
Before you can understand why Jesus died, you need to understand what the problem was that his death solved.

Sin, in the biblical framework, is not primarily about breaking a list of rules. It is about the rupture of a relationship — a turning away from the God who made us and sustains us, choosing to live as if we were the centre of our own story. Every human being, without exception, has done this. Paul makes this explicit in Romans 3:23: 'All have sinned and fall short of the glory of God.'

The consequence of this rupture is death — not merely physical death but separation from God, who is the source of all life. This is not an arbitrary punishment. It is the natural consequence of cutting yourself off from the only source of life that exists.

Here is the problem: God is perfectly just. He cannot simply ignore or overlook sin without his justice being meaningless. But God is also perfectly loving — he does not want the creatures he made to be separated from him forever.

The cross is the only solution that satisfies both. Jesus — fully God and fully human — takes the penalty that justice requires. The debt is paid. The rupture is healed. Those who trust in him receive his perfect standing before God, as if credited to their account.
islamic_contrast:
Islam has no doctrine of original sin and no doctrine of atonement — and these two absences are directly connected.

In Islamic theology, each person is born morally clean and accountable only for their own deeds. Adam's sin affected Adam; it does not fundamentally alter the nature or standing of his descendants. There is no inherited rupture to repair, no deep wound in human nature that needs healing from outside.

The result is a salvation framework that is entirely transactional: do enough good, avoid enough evil, and hope Allah's mercy tips the scales in your favour. No one — including Muhammad — can know in advance whether they will pass.

This raises a genuine question: if there is no real penalty for sin that needs satisfying, and no fundamental brokenness in human nature that needs restoring, what exactly is the Resurrection supposed to have accomplished? The Islamic Jesus — sinless, born of a virgin, taken up to God — performs no salvific function. He is an honoured prophet who saves no one.

The Christian framework, by contrast, makes the cross and Resurrection load-bearing. Remove them and the whole structure collapses — which is exactly why Paul says what he does in 1 Corinthians 15.
